title: Driven by Wonder: The Curious Minds That Unlock Nature's Secrets
title_zh: 好奇驱动：解锁自然之谜的科学家们
intro_zh: 本文约 1000 词，讲述好奇心如何引导科学家做出重大发现，涉及细菌学、天体物理学和动物行为学等领域的真实故事。适合 CET-4 水平阅读。

paragraphs.en: What makes a scientist? Is it a high IQ, years of training, or access to fancy equipment? While these help, the most curious scientists often share a different quality: an insatiable wonder about the world. History shows that many groundbreaking discoveries began not with a grand plan, but with a simple question — a 'why' or a 'what if' that refused to go away.
paragraphs.zh: 是什么造就了科学家？是高智商、多年的训练，还是昂贵的设备？尽管这些都有帮助，但最具好奇心的科学家往往拥有另一种品质：对世界永不知足的好奇。历史表明，许多突破性发现并非始于宏伟计划，而是源于一个简单的问题——一个挥之不去的‘为什么’或‘如果……会怎样’。

paragraphs.en: Consider the story of Barry Marshall, an Australian doctor in the 1980s. At that time, the medical community firmly believed that stomach ulcers were caused by stress and spicy food. But Marshall was puzzled by patients whose ulcers persisted despite a bland diet. He noticed a curve-shaped bacterium in ulcer samples and wondered: could a germ actually cause ulcers? His idea was laughed at. To prove his point, he famously drank a flask of bacteria-laden liquid. He soon developed gastritis, and then successfully cured himself with antibiotics. His curiosity overturned decades of medical dogma, earning him a Nobel Prize.
paragraphs.zh: 想想澳大利亚医生巴里·马歇尔的故事。20世纪80年代，医学界坚信胃溃疡是由压力和辛辣食物引起的。但马歇尔对某些患者即使吃清淡饮食后溃疡仍不消退感到困惑。他在溃疡样本中注意到一种弯曲的细菌，并好奇：细菌真的能引起溃疡吗？他的想法遭到嘲笑。为了证明自己，他喝下了一瓶充满细菌的液体。他很快患上了胃炎，随后用抗生素成功治愈了自己。他的好奇心推翻了数十年的医学教条，并赢得了诺贝尔奖。

paragraphs.en: Similarly, the story of the cosmic microwave background radiation (CMB) began with persistence born from curiosity. In 1964, two radio astronomers, Arno Penzias and Robert Wilson, were testing a sensitive horn antenna. They kept picking up a faint, annoying hiss no matter where they pointed their antenna. They even cleaned out pigeon droppings thinking that was the source of the noise. It wasn't until a curious physicist explained that the hiss was the 'afterglow' of the Big Bang that they realized they had stumbled upon one of the greatest discoveries in cosmology. Their willingness to investigate an annoyance changed our understanding of the universe.
paragraphs.zh: 同样，宇宙微波背景辐射的故事始于好奇心催生的坚持。1964年，两位射电天文学家阿诺·彭齐亚斯和罗伯特·威尔逊正在测试一个灵敏的喇叭形天线。无论天线指向何处，他们总是收到一种微弱的、恼人的嘶嘶声。他们甚至清除了鸽子粪便，以为那是噪音的来源。直到一位好奇的物理学家解释说，这嘶嘶声是宇宙大爆炸的‘余辉’，他们才意识到自己偶然发现了宇宙学中最伟大的发现之一。他们愿意调查一个烦恼，改变了我们对宇宙的理解。

paragraphs.en: Curiosity also drives scientists who study animal behavior. Jane Goodall, for instance, went to Gombe, Tanzania, with no formal scientific training but a burning desire to understand chimpanzees. She observed them for hours, day after day. Her patience paid off when she saw a chimpanzee strip leaves from a twig and use it to fish termites from a mound — a tool! At the time, tool use was considered a uniquely human trait. Goodall's deserved fame came from her willingness to simply watch and ask: 'What are they doing, and why?'.
paragraphs.zh: 好奇心也驱动着研究动物行为的科学家。例如，简·古道尔没有正式的科学训练，却怀着了解黑猩猩的强烈渴望去了坦桑尼亚的贡贝。她日复一日地观察它们数小时。她的耐心得到了回报：她看到一只黑猩猩从树枝上剥下叶子，用它从蚁丘里钓白蚁——工具！当时，使用工具被认为是人类独有的特征。古道尔应得的声誉源于她愿意简单地观察并提问：‘它们在做什么，为什么？’

paragraphs.en: In today's fast-paced world, curiosity often gets overlooked. We are trained to focus on exams, grades, and career goals. Yet, it is curiosity that fuels innovation and incredible discoveries. When we stop asking questions, we stop learning. The most successful scientists are those who hold on to the childlike sense of wonder — they are never satisfied with simple answers.
paragraphs.zh: 在当今快节奏的世界里，好奇心常常被忽视。我们被训练去专注于考试、分数和职业目标。然而，正是好奇心推动了创新和令人难以置信的发现。当我们停止提问时，我们就停止了学习。最成功的科学家是那些保持孩子般好奇心的人——他们从不满足于简单的答案。

paragraphs.en: Moreover, curiosity is not limited to professional scientists. Anyone can cultivate it. You can start by asking more questions about everyday things: How does my phone know where I am? Why is the sky blue? Why do onions make me cry? These small inquiries can lead to a deeper understanding of the world. And who knows? One of your 'silly' questions might someday lead to a breakthrough.
paragraphs.zh: 此外，好奇心并不局限于专业科学家。任何人都可以培养它。你可以从对日常事物提出更多问题开始：我的手机怎么知道我在哪里？为什么天空是蓝色的？为什么洋葱让我流泪？这些小问题可以引导你对世界有更深刻的理解。谁知道呢？你的一个‘愚蠢’问题也许有一天会带来突破。

paragraphs.en: To fuel your curiosity, try keeping a 'wonder journal'. Write down one thing each day that made you curious. It doesn't have to be serious; it could be a weird cloud, a strange sound, or a new word. Then, spend a few minutes researching it. Over time, this habit will train your brain to see the extraordinary in the ordinary.
paragraphs.zh: 为了激发你的好奇心，试着记一本‘好奇日记’。每天记下一件让你感到好奇的事。它不必是严肃的；可以是一朵奇怪的云、一个奇怪的声音或一个新单词。然后，花几分钟研究它。随着时间的推移，这个习惯会训练你的大脑从平凡中看到不平凡。

paragraphs.en: In conclusion, curiosity is the engine of scientific progress. It turns an ordinary person into an explorer. As the famous scientist Albert Einstein once said, 'The important thing is not to stop questioning.' So, nurture your curiosity. Ask 'why' and 'what if'. You never know what secret of the universe you might uncover next.
paragraphs.zh: 总之，好奇心是科学进步的引擎。它把普通人变成探索者。正如著名科学家阿尔伯特·爱因斯坦所说：‘重要的是不要停止提问。’所以，培养你的好奇心。问‘为什么’和‘如果……会怎样’。你永远不知道你接下来可能会揭示宇宙的什么秘密。

paragraphs.en: Remember, every great discovery began with a curious mind. So, let your curiosity lead the way. It might just change the world.
paragraphs.zh: 记住，每一个伟大的发现都始于一颗好奇的心。所以，让你的好奇心带路。它也许就能改变世界。

questions.q: What is the main idea of the passage?
options: A. Scientists are born with high IQs and training.
B. Curiosity is the key to many scientific discoveries.
C. The discovery of stomach ulcers was accidental.
D. Only professional scientists can make breakthroughs.
questions.explain: 文章主旨是说明好奇心驱动了科学发现，通过多个例子（马歇尔、彭齐亚斯和威尔逊、古道尔）论证。选项B正确。

questions.q: According to the passage, how did Barry Marshall prove his idea about ulcers?
options: A. He analyzed data from many patients.
B. He conducted a large clinical trial.
C. He drank a liquid containing bacteria.
D. He published a paper in a famous journal.
questions.explain: 文章明确指出马歇尔喝下了一瓶含有细菌的液体来证明自己的观点，选项C正确。

questions.q: What can be inferred from the story of Penzias and Wilson?
options: A. They intentionally aimed to discover the CMB.
B. Their discovery was a result of investigating an unexplained noise.
C. They were the first to propose the Big Bang theory.
D. Their antenna was originally designed for CMB detection.
questions.explain: 彭齐亚斯和威尔逊最初是被一个持续存在的嘶嘶声所困扰，在调查这个噪音来源的过程中发现了宇宙微波背景辐射，所以选项B正确。

questions.q: The word 'puzzled' in paragraph 2 is closest in meaning to:
questions.explain: 'puzzled'意为‘困惑的’，与confused意思最为接近。
